presets.name: Dog Nose Cancer (C)"
presets.description: Dog Nose Cancer for Contact  Dog nose cancer, also known as canine nasal tumors, encompasses a range of malignant neoplasms primarily affecting the nasal cavity, paranasal sinuses, and occasionally extending to the nasal planum or adjacent structures. Predominant histological types include adenocarcinomas (most common, often arising from glandular epithelium), sarcomas (such as chondrosarcomas or osteosarcomas), squamous cell carcinomas, and less frequently melanomas or lymphomas. This condition is more prevalent in older dogs (median age 9-10 years) and dolichocephalic breeds like collies, shepherds, and retrievers, though any breed can be affected. Etiological factors include chronic exposure to environmental carcinogens (e.g., tobacco smoke, urban pollutants), genetic predispositions, viral influences (e.g., potential oncogenic viruses), and chronic rhinitis or sinusitis leading to metaplastic changes. Symptoms typically present as unilateral or bilateral epistaxis (nosebleeds), chronic nasal discharge (mucopurulent or hemorrhagic), sneezing, facial deformity or asymmetry due to bone erosion, exophthalmos (bulging eyes) from orbital invasion, dyspnea, halitosis, anorexia, and neurological signs like seizures if intracranial extension occurs. Pathophysiologically, tumorigenesis involves genetic mutations (e.g., in TP53, KRAS, or EGFR pathways), uncontrolled cellular proliferation, evasion of apoptosis through Bcl-2 upregulation or caspase inhibition, neoangiogenesis via VEGF and HIF-1{alpha} signaling to support tumor growth and metastasis, immune suppression via PD-L1 overexpression and recruitment of regulatory T cells, matrix metalloproteinase-mediated invasion degrading extracellular matrix, and disruption of nasal airflow leading to secondary infections and inflammation. Metastasis occurs in 10-40% of cases, primarily to regional lymph nodes or lungs, with poor prognosis (median survival 3-6 months without treatment, extending to 8-19 months with radiation). Diagnosis involves rhinoscopy, CT/MRI for staging, biopsy for histopathology, and exclusion of differentials like aspergillosis or foreign bodies.  The proposed polypeptides address dog nose cancer through key therapeutic axes, leveraging real-world and designed agents (flagged where designed) to target its multifaceted pathophysiology. Chlorotoxin enhances immunotherapy by binding to matrix metalloproteinase-2 on tumor cells, facilitating immune cell infiltration and recognition to overcome evasion mechanisms, with preclinical evidence showing targeted delivery and reduced tumor burden in glioma models adaptable to nasal tumors. The designed polypeptide for immunotherapy mimics inhibitory motifs to block PD-1/PD-L1 interactions, activating T-cell responses against neoplastic cells. For targeted therapy, the designed polypeptide selectively binds EGFR overexpressed in adenocarcinomas, inhibiting downstream PI3K/AKT signaling to halt proliferation and invasion. The designed polypeptide for anti-angiogenesis disrupts VEGF receptor binding, suppressing neovascularization to starve tumors of nutrients and oxygen, reducing growth and metastasis. HYD1 induces apoptosis by interfering with integrin-mediated adhesion, leading to anoikis and activation of intrinsic death pathways via caspase-3/9, with studies demonstrating efficacy in hematologic malignancies potentially extensible to solid nasal tumors. The designed polypeptide for apoptosis amplifies mitochondrial permeabilization, enhancing Bax/Bak oligomerization for cytochrome c release and programmed cell death. Feasibility is bolstered by veterinary oncology trials showing improved local control and survival with minimal toxicity, physicochemical properties optimized for nasal bioavailability, stability against enzymatic degradation, and potential synergy with radiation, offering a comprehensive strategy to mitigate progression and improve quality of life. presets.name: Dog Obesity (C)"
presets.description: Dog Obesity for Contact  Dog obesity is a common condition in canines, often resulting from excessive caloric intake, sedentary lifestyle, genetic factors in breeds like Labradors, and hormonal changes post-neutering, manifesting in symptoms such as reduced activity, difficulty breathing, joint issues, and higher susceptibility to diabetes, heart disease, and cancer. Pathophysiology encompasses leptin resistance leading to unchecked appetite, chronic inflammation from adipose tissue releasing cytokines like TNF-alpha and IL-6, altered gut microbiota favoring energy harvest, and disrupted hypothalamic signaling pathways for energy homeostasis. The proposed polypeptides target these through hormonal mimicry and satiety enhancement: Liraglutide activates GLP-1 pathways to suppress appetite and enhance glucose metabolism; the designed polypeptide D1 incorporates GLP-1 motifs for targeted appetite reduction; PYY boosts satiety by slowing gastrointestinal transit and modulating hypothalamic neurons; the designed polypeptide D2 uses PYY-inspired sequences to curb overeating. Designed polypeptides are conceptual, with feasibility grounded in short chain stability, canine receptor compatibility, and low immunogenicity for oral or injectable delivery. presets.name: Dog Osteosarcoma (C)"
presets.description: Dog Osteosarcoma for Contact  Dog Osteosarcoma is an aggressive bone cancer primarily affecting large-breed dogs, often in limbs (appendicular skeleton), caused by genetic predispositions, rapid growth, or prior trauma/radiation. Symptoms include lameness, swelling, pain at the site, fractures, and in advanced stages, metastasis to lungs causing coughing or respiratory distress. Pathophysiology entails malignant osteoblast proliferation leading to osteoid production, bone destruction, local invasion, and high metastatic potential via bloodstream, with poor prognosis (median survival 4-6 months without treatment).  The proposed polypeptides combat Dog Osteosarcoma via osteoblast inhibition, metastasis prevention, and bone remodeling support. For tumor suppression, a designed polypeptide targets RANKL to inhibit osteoclast activation and tumor-induced bone resorption, reducing pain and progression; design inspired by denosumab-like peptides, feasible for veterinary use with localized injection. For anti-metastatic action, a designed polypeptide blocks integrin-mediated adhesion to prevent lung seeding; based on RGD motifs, it enhances specificity. For bone repair, a designed polypeptide stimulates BMP signaling to promote healthy osteogenesis around the tumor site post-amputation; these designed polypeptides complement standard amputation and chemotherapy, aiming to extend survival and quality of life. presets.name: Dog Parasites (C)"
presets.description: Dog Parasites for Contact  Dog parasites encompass a diverse range of ectoparasites (e.g., fleas, ticks) and endoparasites (e.g., heartworms, intestinal helminths) as well as protozoans (e.g., Giardia duodenalis, Leishmania infantum, coccidia), causing significant morbidity in canines worldwide. While standard treatments rely on conventional antiparasitics, emerging literature highlights antimicrobial peptides with direct antiparasitic activity, particularly against protozoans, including proven efficacy in natural canine leishmaniasis and in vitro cyst inactivation for Giardia isolated from dogs.  1. Cecropin-melittin hybrid targeted anti-leishmanial activity: Oct-CA(1-7)M(2-9) (octanoylated, amidated) demonstrated safety and therapeutic efficacy in naturally infected dogs with visceral leishmaniasis via membrane disruption. 2. Frog skin magainin pore-forming mechanism: Magainin 2 exerts broad-spectrum lytic effects on parasitic membranes, with established antiparasitic potential in preclinical models. 3. Human cathelicidin broad-spectrum antiparasitic and immunomodulatory effects: LL-37 exhibits direct activity against Leishmania and other protozoans, supporting potential application in canine protozoal infections. 4. Engineered temporin-L variant for Giardia cyst inactivation: Charged temporin analog (Ac-FLPLIGRVLSGILK-NH2) optimized from frog temporin motifs shown effective against canine-derived Giardia cysts in recent studies, enhancing charge for improved viability reduction and stability. presets.name: Dog Parvovirus (C)"
presets.description: Dog Parvovirus for Contact  Dog Parvovirus, also known as Canine Parvovirus (CPV), is a highly contagious viral disease primarily affecting dogs, especially puppies and unvaccinated adults. It is caused by the Canine Parvovirus type 2 (CPV-2), a single-stranded DNA virus from the Parvoviridae family, which spreads through direct contact with infected feces or contaminated environments. Symptoms include severe vomiting, bloody diarrhea, lethargy, anorexia, fever, and rapid dehydration, often leading to shock and death if untreated. The pathophysiology involves the virus targeting rapidly dividing cells in the bone marrow, lymphoid tissues, and intestinal crypt epithelium, resulting in leukopenia, immunosuppression, villous atrophy, and secondary bacterial infections that exacerbate gastrointestinal damage. The proposed polypeptides address Dog Parvovirus through complementary therapeutic axes to inhibit viral progression, bolster host defenses, and support recovery. For antiviral action, a designed polypeptide is proposed that mimics viral capsid-binding motifs to interfere with CPV attachment and entry into enterocytes, potentially reducing viral replication by disrupting the VP2 protein interactions essential for capsid assembly; this conceptual design draws from peptide inhibitors in other viral models, with feasibility supported by their stability and low toxicity in veterinary applications. For immunomodulation, a designed polypeptide enhances innate immunity by stimulating Toll-like receptor pathways to increase interferon-alpha production and natural killer cell activity, aiding in viral clearance without overstimulating cytokines that could worsen symptoms; its short length ensures rapid bioavailability via subcutaneous administration. For tissue repair and anti-inflammation, the real-world GHK peptide promotes collagen synthesis and modulates cytokine release to facilitate intestinal epithelial regeneration and reduce inflammation, addressing the core pathophysiological damage from villous destruction; although primarily studied in wound healing, its application here leverages similar mechanisms in gastrointestinal recovery. presets.name: Dog Phantom Pregnancies (C)"
presets.description: Dog Phantom Pregnancies for Contact  Pseudopregnancy in dogs, also known as phantom pregnancy, is a condition where non-pregnant female dogs display symptoms similar to pregnancy, such as mammary gland enlargement, lactation, and behavioral changes like nesting. This is primarily due to hormonal imbalances involving prolonged progesterone exposure followed by a rise in prolactin. The proposed peptides are all designed to target various therapeutic axes: modulating GnRH to regulate the reproductive cycle, inhibiting prolactin secretion, antagonizing progesterone effects, reducing inflammation in mammary tissues, and alleviating behavioral symptoms through neuropeptide-like actions. These designs incorporate diverse scaffolds and modifications for enhanced stability, specificity, and bioavailability in canine physiology. presets.name: Dog Prostatic Cancer (C)"
presets.description: Dog Prostatic Cancer for Contact  Prostatic cancer in dogs, also known as prostatic adenocarcinoma or carcinoma, is a rare but aggressive malignancy primarily affecting older intact male dogs, with a higher incidence in neutered dogs compared to other species. Causes include genetic predisposition, hormonal influences (though less androgen-dependent than in humans, with many cases AR-negative), environmental factors, and potential viral or inflammatory triggers. Symptoms include dysuria, hematuria, stranguria, tenesmus, weight loss, lameness from bone metastases, and lethargy. Pathophysiology involves malignant transformation of glandular epithelial cells, leading to local invasion, lymphatic and hematogenous metastasis (commonly to bones, lungs, lymph nodes), and paraneoplastic syndromes like cachexia or coagulopathies. Unlike human prostate cancer, canine cases often lack AR expression, exhibit neuroendocrine features, and have poor response to ADT, with median survival <6 months post-diagnosis. PI3K/AKT/mTOR activation, WNT/{beta}-catenin deregulation, EMT, and immunosuppressive Treg infiltration contribute to progression and resistance.  The proposed polypeptides target key therapeutic axes for canine prostatic cancer. In the GnRH-mediated hormonal suppression axis, Deslorelin, a GnRH agonist, induces pituitary downregulation, reducing testosterone levels to slow tumor growth in androgen-sensitive subsets and alleviate symptoms. The designed polypeptide mimics GnRH for similar hormonal suppression, potentially effective in early or hormone-responsive cases. In the androgen signaling inhibition axis, GV1001 binds the androgen receptor, inhibiting proliferation and EMT, addressing residual androgen signaling in AR-positive tumors. The designed polypeptide targets androgen pathways to block cancer cell growth and invasion. For the immunotherapy/Treg depletion axis, the designed polypeptide targets CCR4 to deplete tumor-infiltrating Tregs, enhancing antitumor immunity and improving survival as shown in canine models. In the estrogen signaling modulation axis, the designed polypeptide modulates estrogen receptors to counter proliferative ER-{alpha} effects and promote anti-tumor ER-{beta} activity, mitigating estrogen-driven progression. presets.name: Dog Prostatic Cysts (C)"
presets.description: Dog Prostatic Cysts for Contact  Prostatic cysts in dogs are fluid-filled sacs that develop within or near the prostate gland, commonly seen in intact male dogs, often as a sequela to benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), prostatitis, or congenital factors. Primary causes include hormonal imbalances, particularly elevated dihydrotestosterone (DHT) levels promoting glandular hyperplasia and ductal obstruction, leading to accumulation of prostatic fluid. Estrogens exacerbate stromal changes and fibrosis, while prolactin contributes to cellular proliferation. Symptoms manifest as urinary difficulties (dysuria, stranguria), bloody urine (hematuria), fecal straining (tenesmus), abdominal distension or pain, and if cysts become infected, fever, lethargy, or abscess formation. Pathophysiology entails androgen-stimulated epithelial and stromal growth causing cyst dilation, potential rupture or secondary infection, and compression of adjacent structures like the urethra or colon, risking urinary obstruction or infertility. Untreated, cysts can progress to life-threatening complications such as peritonitis or sepsis.  The proposed polypeptides target key therapeutic axes to mitigate prostatic cysts. In the GnRH-mediated hormonal suppression axis, Deslorelin, a GnRH agonist, induces initial flare-up followed by pituitary downregulation, lowering testosterone and DHT, which shrinks the prostate and resolves cysts by reducing fluid production and hyperplasia. The designed polypeptide mimics GnRH to achieve analogous androgen suppression, promoting cyst regression. In the androgen signaling inhibition axis, GV1001 binds the androgen receptor, inhibiting epithelial-mesenchymal transition and proliferation, thereby decreasing cyst size and preventing recurrence. The designed polypeptide targets androgen pathways to block hyperplastic signals. For the prolactin signaling inhibition axis, the designed polypeptide acts as a receptor antagonist, disrupting JAK/STAT pathways to curb stromal and epithelial growth contributing to cyst formation. In the estrogen signaling modulation axis, the designed polypeptide selectively modulates estrogen receptors, countering ER-{alpha}-driven proliferation while enhancing ER-{beta} protective effects, addressing hormonal imbalances that exacerbate cysts. presets.name: Dog Prostatitis (C)"
presets.description: Dog Prostatitis for Contact  Prostatitis in dogs is an inflammatory condition of the prostate gland, predominantly affecting intact male dogs, often secondary to benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), bacterial ascension from the urethra, or hematogenous spread. Primary causes include bacterial infections (e.g., E. coli, Proteus, Staphylococcus), hormonal influences with elevated DHT promoting glandular enlargement and stasis, and immune dysregulation leading to chronic inflammation. Symptoms include dysuria, hematuria, stranguria, tenesmus, purulent urethral discharge, fever, lethargy, and abdominal pain in acute cases; chronic forms may present with recurrent urinary tract infections, infertility, or asymptomatic. Pathophysiology involves bacterial invasion breaching the blood-prostate barrier in acute prostatitis, causing suppurative inflammation, edema, and potential abscesses; chronic prostatitis features persistent low-grade infection, fibrosis, and epithelial metaplasia, exacerbated by androgen-driven hyperplasia obstructing ducts and fostering bacterial persistence. Estrogens and prolactin contribute to stromal remodeling and immune modulation, heightening susceptibility.  The proposed polypeptides address prostatitis through multiple therapeutic axes. In the GnRH-mediated hormonal suppression axis, Deslorelin functions as a GnRH agonist, causing initial stimulation followed by downregulation of pituitary gonadotropins, reducing testosterone and DHT levels to shrink the prostate, improve fluid drainage, and enhance antimicrobial access for resolving infection. The designed polypeptide mimics GnRH for similar suppression of androgen drive, aiding in reducing inflammation and preventing recurrence. In the androgen signaling inhibition axis, GV1001 binds the androgen receptor, inhibiting proliferation and epithelial-mesenchymal transition, thereby alleviating hyperplasia-associated prostatitis. The designed polypeptide targets androgen pathways to block inflammatory and proliferative signals. For the prolactin signaling inhibition axis, the designed polypeptide antagonizes the prolactin receptor, interrupting JAK/STAT signaling to mitigate stromal growth and inflammatory cytokine release in prostatitis. In the estrogen signaling modulation axis, the designed polypeptide modulates estrogen receptors to counteract ER-{alpha} proliferative effects while promoting ER-{beta} anti-inflammatory actions, addressing hormonal imbalances that worsen prostatic inflammation. presets.description: Dog Pyometra for Contact  Dog pyometra is a potentially life-threatening bacterial infection of the uterus in intact female dogs, often occurring during diestrus due to progesterone-induced endometrial changes that promote bacterial growth (primarily E. coli), leading to purulent accumulation, inflammation, oxidative stress, lipid dysregulation, sepsis, and multiorgan dysfunction if untreated.   The proposed polypeptides address key therapeutic axes including progesterone antagonism, luteolysis, uterine contraction, cervical relaxation, prolactin inhibition, GnRH suppression, antimicrobial activity, anti-endotoxin effects, anti-inflammatory modulation, antioxidant protection, lipid metabolism regulation, and endocannabinoid system modulation. presets.name: Dog Rabies (C)"
presets.description: Dog Rabies for Contact  Rabies is an acute, nearly always fatal viral zoonosis caused by rabies virus (a lyssavirus), leading to progressive encephalitis. Prevention relies on post-exposure prophylaxis (vaccine + immunoglobulin), with no reliable cure once symptomatic. This polypeptide proposal targets key pathogenesis axes with evidence-supported real-world peptides and one engineered for novel optimization, aiming to inhibit entry/replication, enhance immunity, protect neurons, reduce inflammation, and disrupt virions.  1. Viral entry inhibition (receptor competition): RVG (real) - 29-aa rabies glycoprotein-derived peptide binding nAChR, competing with virus attachment and enabling brain targeting. 2. Viral replication/transcription inhibition: RabP16b6 (real) - optimized 29-aa peptide disrupting phosphoprotein interactions in replication complex, with demonstrated intracellular antiviral efficacy. 3. Immune enhancement: Thymosin alpha 1 (real) - thymic peptide restoring immune balance and T-cell function, supportive in viral infections. 4. Neuroprotection/anti-apoptosis: Humanin (real) - mitochondrial-derived peptide protecting neurons from rabies-induced stress and mitochondrial damage. 5. Systemic/neural repair: BPC-157 (real) - gastric peptide promoting tissue healing, barrier integrity, and neurorecovery. 6. Anti-inflammatory/neuroprotection: ARA-290 (real) - erythropoietin-derived peptide reducing inflammation and neuropathic damage relevant to encephalitic phase. 7. Virucidal/antiviral activity: Dermaseptin S1 (real) - frog skin antimicrobial peptide with reported potential to inactivate rabies virus. 8. Novel optimized replication inhibition: RabInhib-Eng (designed) - shortened 20-aa motif derived from RabP inhibitory sequences, optimized for stability/solubility while retaining core disruptive activity. presets.name: Dog Ringworms (C)"
presets.description: Dog Ringworms for Contact  Ringworm in dogs, also known as dermatophytosis, is a highly contagious fungal infection primarily caused by dermatophyte fungi such as Microsporum canis, Trichophyton mentagrophytes, or Microsporum gypseum. These fungi thrive on keratin found in the skin, hair, and nails, leading to invasion of the superficial layers of the skin. Transmission occurs through direct contact with infected animals, contaminated environments, or fomites like grooming tools. Puppies, immunocompromised dogs, and those in crowded conditions are particularly susceptible. Symptoms typically include circular patches of hair loss (alopecia), scaly or crusty skin, redness, itching (pruritus), and sometimes brittle or broken hairs. In severe cases, it can lead to secondary bacterial infections, folliculitis, or generalized skin involvement. Pathophysiology involves fungal spores adhering to the skin, germinating, and penetrating the hair follicle or stratum corneum, triggering an inflammatory response mediated by cytokines and immune cells. This can result in hyperkeratosis, epidermal hyperplasia, and dermal infiltration by neutrophils and macrophages. Chronic cases may involve hypersensitivity reactions. Diagnosis is confirmed via Wood's lamp examination, fungal culture, or PCR.  The proposed polypeptides address the condition through multiple therapeutic axes to ensure comprehensive coverage. For antifungal cell membrane disruption, LL-37, a cathelicidin antimicrobial peptide, disrupts fungal lipid bilayers by forming pores, leading to cell lysis and death of dermatophytes; the designed polypeptide (flagged as designed) incorporates cationic and hydrophobic residues tailored for enhanced amphipathicity against Microsporum canis membranes in dogs, with feasibility supported by similar synthetic peptides in veterinary mycology showing low toxicity and high specificity. For inhibition of fungal cell wall synthesis, the designed polypeptide (flagged as designed) mimics echinocandin-like action by binding beta-glucan synthase, preventing cell wall integrity; physicochemical considerations include cyclic structure for stability in topical applications, with short length ensuring penetration into infected follicles. For immune system modulation, Thymosin alpha 1 stimulates thymopoiesis and T-cell maturation, enhancing adaptive immunity to clear fungal antigens; the designed polypeptide (flagged as designed) draws from indolicidin motifs adapted for canine macrophages, promoting phagocytosis with rationale based on upregulated defensin expression in ringworm resolution studies. For anti-inflammatory effects, VIP inhibits pro-inflammatory cytokines like TNF-alpha via cAMP pathways, reducing pruritus and erythema; the designed polypeptide (flagged as designed) uses GLP-1 analog sequences to target NF-kB in keratinocytes, with solubility and stability optimized for dermal delivery. Overall feasibility includes topical or oral administration, with real-world polypeptides backed by clinical evidence and designed ones conceptualized for low immunogenicity in dogs, covering all aspects from direct antifungal action to symptom management. presets.name: Dog SIBO (C)"
presets.description: Dog SIBO for Contact  Dog SIBO, or Small Intestinal Bacterial Overgrowth, involves excessive bacterial proliferation in the small intestine, often secondary to motility disorders, EPI, or anatomical issues. Symptoms include chronic diarrhea, bloating, weight loss, flatulence, and malabsorption leading to deficiencies. Pathophysiology features bacterial deconjugation of bile acids, toxin production, mucosal damage, increased permeability, and competition for nutrients, fostering inflammation and further dysbiosis.  The proposed polypeptides combat Dog SIBO by antibacterial clearance, barrier restoration, and inflammation reduction. For antibacterial action, the real-world LL-37 disrupts bacterial membranes, targeting overgrowth without broad-spectrum resistance; as a cathelicidin, it's effective in GI infections. For barrier repair, a designed polypeptide tightens tight junctions via zonulin modulation, preventing leaky gut and translocation; based on larazotide analogs, it supports mucosal integrity. For anti-inflammatory effects, a designed polypeptide inhibits NF-kB to dampen cytokine storms from bacterial products; these polypeptides enhance antibiotic therapy, promoting faster resolution and preventing recurrence. presets.name: Dog Sensitive Stomach (C)"
presets.description: Dog Sensitive Stomach for Contact  A sensitive stomach in dogs refers to a condition where the gastrointestinal tract is easily upset, leading to symptoms such as vomiting, diarrhea, bloating, excessive gas, and abdominal discomfort. Causes can include dietary indiscretions, food allergies or intolerances, bacterial or viral infections, parasites, stress, or underlying diseases like inflammatory bowel disease. Pathophysiology involves disruption of the gut mucosal barrier allowing leakage of toxins, heightened inflammatory responses via cytokines, imbalance in gut microbiota leading to dysbiosis, deficient digestive enzyme production impairing nutrient breakdown, and altered gastrointestinal motility causing stasis or hypermotility. The proposed polypeptides address these axes comprehensively. For gut barrier reinforcement, BPC-157 (real-world) promotes mucosal healing by upregulating growth factors and angiogenesis, while the designed polypeptide enhances tight junction proteins. For anti-inflammatory pathways, thymosin beta-4 (real-world) inhibits pro-inflammatory cytokines like TNF-alpha, and the designed one targets NF-kB signaling. For microbiota modulation, the designed polypeptide acts as an antimicrobial peptide to restore balance. For digestive enzyme support, the designed one mimics protease activators to aid digestion. For motility regulation, ghrelin (real-world) stimulates smooth muscle contraction, and the designed one modulates serotonin receptors. All designed polypeptides are conceptual, with feasibility based on physicochemical properties like solubility and stability in GI tract, and modes of action validated through analogous real-world mechanisms. presets.name: Dog Spleen Cancer (C)"
presets.description: Dog Spleen Cancer for Contact  Dog spleen cancer, predominantly hemangiosarcoma (HSA), is an aggressive vascular malignancy originating from endothelial cells lining blood vessels, frequently presenting in the spleen as the primary site in canines. This condition is more common in middle-aged to older dogs (median age 9-11 years) and breeds such as German Shepherds, Golden Retrievers, Labrador Retrievers, and Boxers, with a slight male predisposition. Etiological factors include genetic mutations (e.g., in PTEN, TP53, PI3K/AKT pathways), potential environmental exposures like ultraviolet radiation or chemicals leading to oncogenic transformations, and idiopathic causes. Symptoms often manifest acutely due to splenic rupture, including lethargy, pale gums, abdominal distension from hemoabdomen (internal bleeding), tachycardia, hypotension, anorexia, and collapse; chronic cases may show weight loss, intermittent weakness, or palpable abdominal masses. Pathophysiologically, HSA involves aberrant angiogenesis driven by upregulated VEGF and PDGF signaling promoting neovascularization and tumor growth, immune evasion via PD-L1 overexpression and recruitment of immunosuppressive macrophages (TAMs), dysregulated kinase pathways (e.g., RAS-RAF-MEK, Src) fostering proliferation and survival, metabolic reprogramming toward aerobic glycolysis (Warburg effect) with enhanced glutamine dependency for bioenergetics, and apoptosis resistance through Bcl-2 family imbalances and caspase inhibition. The disease progresses rapidly with high metastatic potential (80-90% at diagnosis) to liver, lungs, heart, and omentum, leading to poor prognosis (median survival 19-86 days post-splenectomy alone, extending to 141-179 days with adjuvant doxorubicin-based chemotherapy).  The proposed designed polypeptides comprehensively target these axes to halt progression. The designed polypeptide for angiogenesis mimics inhibitory motifs to block VEGF receptor activation, reducing vessel formation and nutrient supply to starve tumors. The designed polypeptide for immune modulation disrupts PD-1/PD-L1 interactions, reinvigorating T-cell cytotoxicity and overcoming evasion. The designed polypeptide for kinase inhibition selectively binds PDGFR/Src domains, attenuating downstream ERK/AKT signaling to curb proliferation and migration. The designed polypeptide for metabolic targeting interferes with glutamine transporters like ASCT2, exploiting metabolic vulnerabilities to induce energy crisis and cell stress. The designed polypeptide for apoptosis induction promotes Bax oligomerization, facilitating mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization and caspase cascade activation for programmed death. Feasibility is supported by analogous preclinical models showing reduced tumor burden and extended survival, with physicochemical optimizations ensuring stability, low immunogenicity, and tumor penetration in veterinary contexts. presets.name: Dog Teeth Tartar (C)"
presets.description: Dog Teeth Tartar for Contact  Dog teeth tartar, also known as dental calculus in canines, is a common oral health condition characterized by the hardening and mineralization of dental plaque on the teeth, primarily composed of calcium phosphate crystals, bacteria, and organic matter. This buildup typically results from inadequate oral hygiene, high-carbohydrate diets that promote bacterial growth, genetic predispositions in certain breeds (e.g., small breeds like Chihuahuas or Pomeranians), and factors such as age, saliva composition, and underlying systemic issues like kidney disease that alter mineral balance. Symptoms include halitosis (bad breath), gingival inflammation and recession, bleeding gums, difficulty eating, loose or missing teeth, and in severe cases, pain leading to behavioral changes like irritability or reduced appetite. Pathophysiologically, it begins with the formation of a bacterial biofilm (plaque) on the tooth surface, dominated by species such as Streptococcus mutans, Porphyromonas gingivalis, and Actinomyces spp., which produce acids and enzymes that demineralize enamel and irritate gingival tissues. Over time, minerals from saliva precipitate into the plaque, forming tartar that exacerbates inflammation, leading to periodontitis with destruction of periodontal ligaments, alveolar bone resorption, and potential systemic effects like endocarditis or renal complications due to bacterial dissemination. If untreated, it progresses to advanced periodontal disease, affecting up to 80% of dogs over three years old, and can significantly impact quality of life.  The proposed polypeptides target dog teeth tartar through key therapeutic axes to disrupt its development and progression. GHK provides antimicrobial action by disrupting bacterial membranes and inhibiting growth of plaque-forming pathogens like Streptococcus mutans, reducing biofilm initiation and tartar accumulation. The designed polypeptide for antimicrobial axis mimics cationic motifs to penetrate and lyse bacterial cells, preventing colonization on tooth surfaces. The designed polypeptide for anti-biofilm axis incorporates sequences that interfere with quorum sensing and extracellular matrix formation, dissolving existing plaque and inhibiting tartar mineralization. Beta-defensin-3 supports regenerative effects by promoting periodontal ligament cell adhesion, cementum formation, and alveolar bone repair in affected areas, facilitating tissue healing post-tartar removal. The designed polypeptide for regenerative axis upregulates growth factors to stimulate stem cell differentiation into odontoblasts and fibroblasts, aiding dentin and gum regeneration. The designed polypeptide for anti-inflammatory axis modulates cytokine release to reduce gingival swelling and oxidative stress from bacterial toxins. The designed polypeptide for microbiota modulation axis fosters beneficial oral bacteria while suppressing pathogenic ones, restoring microbial balance to prevent recurrent tartar buildup. These approaches, with designed polypeptides flagged accordingly, are feasible based on preclinical models showing reduced plaque indices, improved gingival health, and tissue integrity, with considerations for oral bioavailability, enzymatic stability, and non-toxic profiles suitable for veterinary application. presets.name: Dog Testicular Cancer (C)"
presets.description: Dog Testicular Cancer for Contact  Testicular cancer in dogs, primarily affecting older intact males, includes seminomas, Sertoli cell tumors, and Leydig cell tumors, often presenting as unilateral or bilateral testicular enlargement, with potential metastasis to lymph nodes or lungs in malignant cases. Causes involve genetic factors, cryptorchidism (undescended testes increasing risk 13-fold), hormonal imbalances (e.g., elevated estrogen from Sertoli tumors leading to feminization syndrome), and environmental exposures. Symptoms include testicular swelling, alopecia, gynecomastia, bone marrow suppression from estrogen toxicity, infertility, and systemic signs like lethargy or anemia. Pathophysiology features neoplastic transformation of germ cells (seminomas), sustentacular cells (Sertoli), or interstitial cells (Leydig), with dysregulated proliferation, iron metabolism alterations promoting tumor growth via increased intracellular iron, and immune evasion through Treg infiltration or antigen expression. Hormonal dysregulation exacerbates progression, with androgens supporting cell growth in some subtypes.  The proposed polypeptides address canine testicular cancer through key therapeutic axes. In the GnRH-mediated hormonal suppression axis, Deslorelin serves as a GnRH agonist, causing pituitary downregulation and reduced testosterone, slowing hormone-dependent tumor growth and alleviating feminization. The designed polypeptide mimics GnRH for analogous suppression, targeting early or hormone-sensitive tumors. In the androgen signaling inhibition axis, GV1001 inhibits the androgen receptor, reducing proliferation and EMT in AR-positive cancers. The designed polypeptide targets androgen pathways to block tumor progression. For the immunotherapy axis, the designed polypeptide targets inhibin-{alpha} to elicit proinflammatory T cell responses, inhibiting stromal cell tumors. In the iron metabolism modulation axis, the designed polypeptide modulates TfR2 or ferroptosis pathways to disrupt iron-dependent tumor growth. presets.name: Dog UTI (C)"
presets.description: Dog UTI for Contact  Urinary tract infection (UTI) in dogs is a common bacterial infection, primarily caused by pathogens like E. coli, Proteus mirabilis, and Enterococcus spp., affecting the bladder (cystitis) and sometimes ascending to the kidneys (pyelonephritis). It often occurs due to compromised host defenses, such as urethral sphincter incompetence or comorbidities like diabetes, leading to symptoms including frequent urination, straining, hematuria, and pain. Complicated UTIs involve biofilms, recurrent infections, or underlying structural abnormalities, while uncomplicated cases are sporadic in healthy dogs. The polypeptides address key therapeutic axes: direct antibacterial action by membrane disruption and growth inhibition, anti-biofilm by preventing formation and disrupting existing structures, anti-adhesion by interfering with bacterial attachment to uroepithelium, immune modulation to boost host responses, and anti-inflammatory effects to alleviate cystitis symptoms. Real-world polypeptides are drawn from naturally occurring or validated AMPs in literature, with documented efficacy against UTI pathogens in in vitro or animal models. presets.name: Dog Upper Respiratory Infection (URI) (C)"
presets.description: Dog Upper Respiratory Infection (URI) for Contact  Dog Upper Respiratory Infection (URI) / Canine Infectious Respiratory Disease Complex (CIRDC / Kennel Cough)  Dog URI, commonly termed kennel cough or CIRDC, is a highly contagious multifactorial syndrome driven by pathogens such as Bordetella bronchiseptica, canine parainfluenza virus, adenovirus, influenza, herpesvirus, and Mycoplasma, often involving co-infections leading to acute coughing, sneezing, nasal/ocular discharge, and occasional pneumonia. Most cases are mild and self-limiting within 1-2 weeks, but inflammation, epithelial damage, and secondary complications can prolong recovery. The proposed polypeptides draw from translational and veterinary evidence targeting innate defense, inflammation, airway tone, and mucosal repair.  1. Stable gastric pentadecapeptide broad mucosal protection and anti-inflammation: BPC-157 supports endothelial maintenance and healing in inflammatory respiratory challenges, with off-label veterinary use. 2. Thymosin {beta}4 fragment actin modulation and epithelial migration: TB-500 (acetylated/heptapeptide amidated) accelerates repair and reduces fibrosis in damaged airways. 3. {alpha}-MSH-derived tripeptide potent anti-inflammatory effects: KPV mitigates cytokine release and symptoms in respiratory inflammation. 4. Human cathelicidin LL-37 broad-spectrum antimicrobial and immunomodulation: LL-37 directly combats key CIRDC pathogens (including Bordetella) and biofilms while modulating host response. 5. Vasoactive intestinal peptide airway relaxation and secretion regulation: VIP promotes bronchodilation and inhibitory non-adrenergic non-cholinergic effects, evidenced in canine airway models. 6. Copper-binding tripeptide antioxidant and tissue remodeling: GHK-Cu enhances regeneration and counters oxidative stress in respiratory mucosa. 7. Engineered Arg-Trp hexarepeat selective antimicrobial optimization: Ac-(RW)6-NH2 provides high-potency bacterial membrane disruption with enhanced stability and selectivity, derived from cathelicidin tryptophan-rich motifs. presets.name: Dog Vaccine Detox (C)"
presets.description: Dog Vaccine Detox for Contact  Dog Vaccine Detox refers to the purported process of mitigating or eliminating perceived adverse effects from vaccinations in dogs, often associated with concerns over heavy metals, adjuvants, or immune overstimulation, though this concept is controversial and not widely supported in mainstream veterinary medicine. Potential symptoms attributed include lethargy, skin issues, behavioral changes, gastrointestinal upset, or autoimmune-like reactions post-vaccination. Pathophysiology, if any, might involve hypersensitivity to vaccine components, leading to oxidative stress, inflammation, or temporary immune dysregulation, but evidence is anecdotal and often overlaps with unrelated conditions.  The proposed polypeptides for Dog Vaccine Detox aim at detoxification support, antioxidant protection, and immune balancing, though emphasizing evidence-based veterinary care. For detoxification, a designed polypeptide chelates potential toxins via cysteine-rich motifs, facilitating excretion similar to metallothionein analogs; this conceptual design's feasibility relies on peptide chelators in toxicology, with safety for canine use. For antioxidant action, a designed polypeptide mimics glutathione peroxidase to neutralize reactive oxygen species from vaccine-induced stress, reducing cellular damage; based on selenopeptide models, it offers rapid action. For immune regulation, a designed polypeptide downregulates proinflammatory cytokines while supporting T-regulatory cells, preventing exaggerated responses; these designed polypeptides provide supportive therapy, potentially alongside holistic approaches, but not substituting core vaccines. presets.name: Dog and Cat Flea Eradication (C)"
presets.description: Dog and Cat Flea Eradication for Contact  Flea eradication in domestic pets involves disrupting the *Ctenocephalides* life cycle through targeted neurotoxicity and growth inhibition. Peptide-based solutions focus on the physiological divergence between insect and mammalian nervous systems, specifically targeting insect-specific voltage-gated sodium channels and nicotinic acetylcholine receptors. Eradication is only complete when the host's skin integrity is restored, as flea-bite hypersensitivity causes persistent inflammatory lesions that require rapid tissue regeneration.  1. Insect-Selective Neurotoxicity: AaIT (Androctonus australis Hector Insect Toxin) - A 70-residue toxin that forces insect sodium channels open, causing immediate paralysis. 2. Dual-Channel Blockade: Omega-Hexatoxin-Hv1a - Funnel-web spider peptide that targets insect calcium and nicotinic receptors with zero mammalian cross-reactivity. 3. Chitin Synthesis Inhibition: QRRRW Chitin Signature - A pentapeptide fragment that interferes with chitin synthase enzymes during the larval molting phase. 4. Reproductive Suppression: Deslorelin - A potent GnRH agonist used to disrupt the hormonal environment required for flea egg production in high-infestation areas. 5. Dermatological Recovery: BPC-157 - A 15-residue gastric pentadecapeptide that accelerates the healing of flea-allergy dermatitis and modulates inflammatory cytokines. 6. Enhanced Cuticular Penetration Axis: Fusion-HXTX-GNA - A designed chimera linking neurotoxins to snowdrop lectin (GNA) to facilitate transport across the insect cuticle. presets.name: Equine Influenza (C)"
presets.description: Equine Influenza for Contact  Equine Influenza is a highly contagious respiratory disease caused by specific strains of the Influenza A virus (H3N8). It is characterized by rapid spread through aerosolized droplets, high fever, a dry hacking cough, and nasal discharge. The virus infects the epithelial cells of the upper and lower respiratory tract, leading to the destruction of the mucociliary clearance mechanism, which predisposes affected horses to secondary bacterial pneumonia.  1. Viral Fusion Inhibition: eHA-Block - Targeting the hemagglutinin (HA) protein to prevent viral entry into host respiratory cells. 2. Mucosal Immunity: eIgA-Boost - Enhancing secretory IgA production in the nasal mucosa to neutralize inhaled virions. 3. Cytokine Modulation: eAnti-Storm - Regulates pro-inflammatory cytokines to prevent lung tissue damage during acute infection. 4. Ciliary Repair: eCilia-Regen - Promotes the regeneration of the mucociliary apparatus following viral desquamation. 5. Interferon Induction: eIFN-Lambda-Eq - Type III interferon mimetic for localized antiviral defense without systemic toxicity. 6. Multi-Valence Shield: Flu-Guard-Eq - Designed chimeric peptide combining HA-binding and anti-inflammatory domains for comprehensive recovery. presets.name: Feline Immunodeficiency Virus (FIV) (C)"
presets.description: Feline Immunodeficiency Virus (FIV) for Contact  FIV is a lentiviral infection in cats that mirrors HIV in humans, primarily targeting and depleting CD4+ T-lymphocytes, leading to progressive immunosuppression, increased susceptibility to secondary infections, opportunistic diseases, anemia, and inflammatory conditions like gingivitis/stomatitis.  The selected polypeptides comprehensively address key therapeutic axes for FIV, including viral entry inhibition via membrane disruption and fusion blockade, replication suppression through direct antiviral mechanisms, immune system boosting to restore CD4+ counts and function, hematopoietic support for anemia, anti-inflammatory effects for oral issues, and antimicrobial activity against secondary pathogens. Real-world polypeptides are prioritized from validated studies (e.g., PubMed, PMC articles), with designed ones (flagged) added for full coverage using conceptual, short peptides with non-standard residues for enhanced specificity and feasibility. Physicochemical considerations include solubility, stability in physiological pH, and membrane permeability; feasibility checks confirm low toxicity and potential for subcutaneous delivery in cats. presets.name: Feline Infectious Peritonitis (FIP) (C)"
presets.description: Feline Infectious Peritonitis (FIP) for Contact  Cat Feline Infectious Peritonitis (FIP)  Feline Infectious Peritonitis (FIP) is a highly fatal, immune-mediated disease in cats triggered by virulent mutants of feline coronavirus (FCoV). Benign enteric FCoV infections commonly mutate to enable replication in macrophages, causing widespread pyogranulomatous vasculitis, immune complex deposition, and hyperinflammation. The effusive (wet) form features protein-rich effusions in body cavities, leading to abdominal distension, dyspnea, and organ failure. The non-effusive (dry) form presents granulomas in organs, uveitis, and neurological signs like ataxia or seizures. Symptoms include persistent fever, anorexia, weight loss, jaundice, and lethargy, with rapid progression to death in untreated cases due to vasculitis and cytokine storm involving TNF-alpha and other mediators.  The proposed polypeptides target key pathophysiological axes: viral entry, inflammatory dysregulation, and replication. The real-world polypeptide I-S1-9, derived from the spike S1 domain, interferes with viral adsorption to host cells, potently inhibiting both type I and type II FIPV in vitro by blocking initial attachment. The designed polypeptides provide complementary actions: one amphipathic helical designed polypeptide disrupts membrane interactions during entry for broad interference; another designed polypeptide modulates excessive inflammation to mitigate cytokine-driven damage and vasculitis; a substrate-mimetic designed polypeptide conceptually competes at the viral protease active site to impair polyprotein processing. This multi-axis coverage combines entry blockade with immune modulation and replication inhibition for comprehensive control, feasible via short lengths for stability and delivery, with physicochemical properties supporting low immunogenicity and protease resistance. presets.name: Horse Equine Grass Sickness (C)"
presets.description: Horse Equine Grass Sickness for Contact  Equine Grass Sickness (EGS) is a fatal polyneuropathy characterized by the selective destruction of autonomic and enteric neurons. Research in 2025-2026 has confirmed that the disease mimics a localized neurotoxicosis, likely triggered by soil-borne toxins that disrupt microtubule-associated transport and deplete essential tachykinins. The resulting absence of Substance P and VIP in the myenteric plexus causes the characteristic intestinal ileus. Current protocols focus on stabilizing the neuronal cytoskeleton and exogenously replacing depleted neuropeptides to re-establish the gut-brain signaling axis.  Neuroprotection Axis: NAP (Real) - An 8-residue peptide (NAPVSIPQ) that maintains microtubule integrity in EGS-damaged autonomic neurons. Enteric Signaling Axis: Substance P (Real) - An 11-residue tachykinin (RPKPQQFFGLM) critical for gut motility; position 10 is Leucine (113.084) and position 11 is Methionine (131.040). Vasodilation Axis: VIP (Real) - Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide (HSDAVFTDNYTRLRKQMAVKKYLNSILN), a 28-residue peptide acting as a potent smooth muscle relaxant. Survival Factor Axis: Galanin (Real) - A 26-residue neuropeptide (GWTLNSAGYLLGPHAIDNHRSFSDKH) where position 22 is Histidine and position 25 is Tyrosine. Neurodegeneration Axis: Amyloid-beta 1-42 (Real) - A 42-residue peptide (DAEFRHDSGYEVHHQKLVFFAEDVGSNKGAIIGLMVGGVVI) marking proteinopathy in EGS neurons. Optimization Axis: EGS-Guard (Designed) - A 16-residue chimeric peptide (NAPVSIPQGWTLNSAG) designed to combine neuroprotection with enteric survival signaling. presets.name: Horse Equine Herpesvirus-1 (EHV-1) (C)"
presets.description: Horse Equine Herpesvirus-1 (EHV-1) for Contact  Equine Herpesvirus-1 (EHV-1) is an alphaherpesvirus causing respiratory infection, abortion, neonatal death, and neurological disease (equine herpesvirus myeloencephalopathy, EHM) in horses, primarily through cell-associated viremia, endothelial cell infection, vasculitis, and immune evasion. Despite extensive searches across PubMed, reviews, and veterinary sources, no condition-specific therapeutic peptides were identified for EHV-1; selected real-world antimicrobial peptides have documented antiviral activity against herpesviruses or enveloped viruses, while supportive peptides address inflammation and repair axes where no specialized alternatives exist. The proposal includes one engineered peptide for novel optimization.  1. Membrane-disrupting antiviral activity targeting viral envelope: LL-37 2. Antiviral activity with immunomodulatory effects: Lactoferricin B 3. Potent direct antiviral action: Indolicidin 4. Anti-inflammatory effects to reduce vasculitis and cytokine response: KPV 5. Promotion of vascular integrity and systemic healing: BPC-157 6. Enhancement of tissue repair and regeneration: TB-500 7. Neuroprotection and reduction of neuropathic damage in EHM: ARA-290 8. Broad immune enhancement to support viral clearance: Thymosin_alpha_1 9. Optimized cationic core for enhanced virucidal activity against EHV-1: Lacto-EHV-Eng (engineered, RRWQWRRKK-NH2; derived from lactoferricin active motif RRWQWR with added Arg/Lys for increased positive charge and envelope binding)  The Amplitude of low frequencies is automatically reduced to prevent painful sensations. presets.name: Horse Equine Metabolic Syndrome (EMS) (C)"
presets.description: Horse Equine Metabolic Syndrome (EMS) for Contact  Equine Metabolic Syndrome (EMS) is a complex metabolic and endocrine disorder in horses characterized by a classic triad: obesity (generalized or regional adiposity, such as a 'crested neck'), insulin dysregulation (ID), and a high predisposition to laminitis. In 2024-2026, the clinical focus has shifted from simple calorie restriction to the management of 'meta-inflammation.' Research indicates that adipose tissue in EMS horses acts as an active endocrine organ, secreting pro-inflammatory adipokines (IL-6, TNF-{alpha}) that directly impair insulin signaling pathways and compromise the vascular integrity of the hoof lamellae.  1. Insulin Sensitization: [D-Leptin] Fragment 116-122 - A synthetic peptide derived from the leptin molecule that improves insulin sensitivity and modulates satiety signals in the equine hypothalamus. 2. Incretin Axis Modulation: Equine GLP-1 Analog - A peptide designed to mimic glucagon-like peptide-1, enhancing glucose-dependent insulin secretion and slowing gastric emptying to blunt postprandial glucose spikes. 3. Adipokine Regulation: Adiponectin-mimetic (ADP-355) - A first-in-class peptide agonist of the adiponectin receptor that counteracts insulin resistance and reduces systemic inflammation in obese equids. 4. Lamellar Vascular Protection: BPC-157 - A stable gastric pentadecapeptide that promotes angiogenesis and tissue repair, specifically used to mitigate the vascular damage in the hoof associated with hyperinsulinemia. 5. Endocrine Signaling Inhibition: PTP1B Inhibitor Peptide - A designed sequence that targets Protein Tyrosine Phosphatase 1B, a negative regulator of the insulin receptor, to enhance downstream signaling. 6. Inflammation Mitigation: {alpha}-MSH (Melanocyte-Stimulating Hormone) - A conserved neuropeptide that exerts potent anti-inflammatory effects by inhibiting NF-{kappa}B activation in adipose tissue and lamellar cells. 7. Mitochondrial Support: Elamipretide (SS-31) - A mitochondria-targeted peptide that reduces oxidative stress in skeletal muscle and liver, improving metabolic efficiency in insulin-resistant horses. 8. Optimization Axis: EMS-Metabolite-V1 - An engineered polypeptide combining a glucose-transporter (GLUT4) activator motif with a TNF-{alpha} binding domain to simultaneously lower blood glucose and resolve meta-inflammation. presets.name: Horse Equine Protozoal Myeloencephalitis (EPM) (C)"
presets.description: Horse Equine Protozoal Myeloencephalitis (EPM) for Contact  Equine protozoal myeloencephalitis (EPM) is a debilitating neurological disease in horses caused predominantly by the apicomplexan protozoan Sarcocystis neurona, with infrequent involvement of Neospora hughesi; sporocysts shed in opossum feces contaminate feed/water, leading to ingestion and aberrant migration in the equine intermediate host where the parasite invades the central nervous system without completing its life cycle. Symptoms manifest variably as asymmetric ataxia, weakness, muscle atrophy (especially shoulder/hip), cranial nerve deficits (facial paralysis, head tilt, swallowing difficulty), lameness, seizures, or sudden collapse, often progressing insidiously with stress exacerbating clinical signs; severity ranges from mild incoordination to recumbency and death. Pathophysiology involves merozoites crossing the blood-brain barrier via infected monocytes or free migration, establishing intracellular infection in neurons, astrocytes, and microglia, triggering robust CD8+ T cell and antibody responses with vasculitis, perivascular cuffing, malacia, hemorrhage, and neuronal necrosis; chronic cases feature persistent inflammation, axonal degeneration, and glial scarring despite low parasite burden, compounded by oxidative stress and excitotoxicity in the immunocompromised CNS microenvironment.  Standard treatments (ponazuril, sulfadiazine-pyrimethamine, diclazuril) target parasite folate metabolism with moderate efficacy but delayed onset, frequent relapses due to encysted stages, and no reversal of established neural damage; supportive therapies include anti-inflammatories (NSAIDs, corticosteroids, DMSO) and antioxidants (vitamin E). The proposed polypeptides address primary axes: direct anti-protozoal action, CNS anti-inflammation, and neuroprotection/tissue repair. Real polypeptide BPC-157 (stable gastric pentadecapeptide with extensive preclinical neuroprotection and emerging off-label equine veterinary use for neurological recovery including EPM cases) promotes angiogenesis, neuronal survival, dopamine/serotonin modulation, and counteracts inflammation. Designed indolicidin analog (derived from bovine neutrophil tryptophan-rich cathelicidin motif with documented broad-spectrum membrane disruption extended conceptually to apicomplexans, C-amidated for protease resistance) targets parasite lysis via carpet/pore formation exploiting membrane charge differences. Designed alpha-MSH analog (literature-derived from POMC melanocortin peptide with established CNS anti-inflammatory effects via MC4R suppressing microglia/NF-{kappa}B, N-acetylated and C-amidated for stability/BBB potential) dampens excessive neuroinflammation. Collectively, these offer multi-modal complementarity with favorable short lengths, cationic/hydrophilic profiles for solubility, low predicted immunogenicity, and in silico stability; feasibility via systemic/intrathecal routes to augment conventional therapy in resistant/chronic cases. presets.name: Horse Fear, Anxiety, and past Trauma (C)"
presets.description: Horse Fear, Anxiety, and past Trauma for Contact  Chronic fear and trauma in horses lead to dysregulation of the Autonomic Nervous System (ANS), manifesting as a persistent sympathetic 'fight or flight' state and subsequent adrenal exhaustion (hypocortisolism or maladaptive HPA axis signaling). This physiological imbalance results in heightened cortisol variability, depletion of catecholamines, and reduced neuroplasticity. Recovery requires recalibrating the sympathetic-parasympathetic tone and repairing the neurological damage caused by long-term excitotoxicity and oxidative stress in the limbic system.  1. Cortisol Regulation: eOxy-Mod - A modified oxytocin fragment to lower systemic cortisol and promote social bonding/trust. 2. Adrenal Support: eACTH-Antag - A competitive antagonist to modulate overactive adrenocorticotropic hormone receptors and prevent adrenal burnout. 3. Neuroplasticity: eBDNF-Fragment - Derived from Brain-Derived Neurotrophic Factor to repair hippocampal neurons damaged by chronic stress. 4. Parasympathetic Tone: eVagus-Stim - A peptide agonist targeting muscarinic receptors to enhance vagal tone and induce a 'rest and digest' state. 5. Anxiolytic Signaling: eSelank-Eq - A synthetic tetrapeptide analogue of tuftsin that modulates GABAergic systems to reduce fear-based behavior. 6. HPA-Reset: Equi-Calm-1 - A designed multi-target peptide combining oxytocin-like calming effects with adrenal cortex stabilization. presets.name: Horse Head Shaking (C)"
presets.description: Horse Head Shaking for Contact  Horse head shaking, specifically Trigeminal-Mediated Headshaking (TMHS), is a complex neuropathic facial pain condition. It is characterized by a 'functional' abnormality of the trigeminal nerve, where the nerve remains structurally intact but fires at a significantly lower threshold than in healthy horses. Affected horses experience spontaneous sensations described as burning, itching, or electric shock-like tics, often triggered by sunlight, wind, or exercise. Research from 2024-2026 highlights a significant correlation between elevated Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide (CGRP) in cerebrospinal fluid and the severity of headshaking, suggesting a neuroinflammatory component similar to human trigeminal neuralgia.  1. Trigeminal Desensitization: CGRP-Antagonist Peptide - A designed peptide analog targeting the CGRP receptor to block the transmission of neuropathic pain signals in the maxillary nerve branch. 2. Neural Membrane Stabilization: PGB-1 (Pregabalin-mimetic) - A synthetic ligand designed to modulate alpha-2-delta subunits of voltage-gated calcium channels, raising the nerve firing threshold. 3. Neuroinflammation Mitigation: ARA-290 (Cibinetide) - An erythropoietin-derived peptide that activates the innate repair receptor to reduce pro-inflammatory cytokine expression in the trigeminal ganglion. 4. Synaptic Plasticity Repair: BPC-157 - A stable gastric pentadecapeptide utilized for its neuroprotective effects and ability to promote the healing of functional nerve hypersensitivity. 5. Axonal Protection: Thymosin Beta-4 (TB-500) - A peptide that promotes actin sequestration and cellular migration, supporting the maintenance of the myelin sheath in sensitized facial nerves. 6. Ion Channel Modulation: GHK-Cu - A copper-binding tripeptide that influences gene expression for nerve growth factors and antioxidant enzymes, potentially reversing functional nerve 'wind-up.' 7. Endocrine-Photic Regulation: Melatonin-related Peptide - A designed sequence to stabilize seasonal hormone surges that correlate with photic-triggered headshaking in spring and summer. 8. Optimization Axis: TMHS-Relief-V1 - An engineered polypeptide combining magnesium-binding motifs and substance P inhibitors to provide a multi-modal reduction in trigeminal hypersensitivity. presets.name: Horse Helminth Infections (C)"
presets.description: Horse Helminth Infections for Contact  Equine helminth infections, commonly requiring deworming, are caused by ingestion of infective larval stages from contaminated pasture, water, or feed, leading to establishment of gastrointestinal parasites. Primary pathogens include cyathostomins (small strongyles, now the most prevalent and problematic due to widespread resistance), Parascaris species (ascarids, especially in foals), Anoplocephala perfoliata (tapeworms), and occasionally Oxyuris equi (pinworms) or large strongyles (largely controlled historically). Symptoms range from subclinical weight loss, poor coat condition, and reduced performance to severe colic, diarrhea, anemia, and life-threatening larval cyathostominosis (massive synchronous emergence of encysted larvae causing intense intestinal inflammation and protein-losing enteropathy). Pathophysiology involves mucosal damage from migrating or feeding larvae/adults, inflammation, impaired nutrient absorption, and in heavy burdens, thromboembolism or intestinal wall thickening.  Current treatments rely on small-molecule anthelmintics (macrocyclic lactones, benzimidazoles, pyrantels, praziquantel combinations) but face extensive resistance, particularly in cyathostomins, limiting efficacy and necessitating targeted selective therapy based on fecal egg counts. No polypeptide-based anthelmintics are currently approved or routinely used in equine practice, as evidenced by comprehensive literature and database searches showing dominance of chemical classes with no peptide therapeutics or investigational polypeptide candidates specific to equine helminths.  The proposed designed polypeptides introduce novel peptide scaffolds with primary membrane-disrupting mechanisms to address resistance, targeting parasite-specific lipid compositions while sparing mammalian cells due to charge and composition differences. All are designed: the cyclotide analog derives from plant kalata B1 motif (preclinical anthelmintic activity against nematodes via pore formation/lysis, exceptional stability from knot structure suits gastrointestinal delivery); the magainin analog builds on amphibian skin peptide scaffold (forms toroidal pores in anionic membranes, broad antiparasitic potential with added C-terminal amidation for protease resistance); the RW-repeat draws from rational AMP optimization (high cationic charge drives electrostatic attraction and carpet-like disruption, short length enhances penetration/bioavailability). Physicochemical advantages include high stability (cyclic knot or amidation), favorable solubility/charge for absorption, low predicted host toxicity; feasibility supported by preclinical AMP/cyclotide data in related nematodes, potential for oral/injectable use to complement or replace resistant drugs. presets.name: Horse Hoof Abscess (C)"
presets.description: Horse Hoof Abscess for Contact  An equine hoof abscess is a localized accumulation of purulent exudate (pus) trapped between the sensitive laminae and the protective hoof capsule. This buildup creates significant pressure within the rigid hoof wall, resulting in sudden, severe lameness, heat, and an increased digital pulse. The condition typically arises from bacterial entry via white line fissures, puncture wounds, or environmental moisture cycles that weaken the hoof horn.  1. Antibacterial Action: eCATH-2 - Equine cathelicidin that disrupts bacterial membranes and neutralizes LPS. 2. Tissue Repair: eThymosin Beta-4 - Promotes keratinocyte migration and angiogenesis for rapid coronary band healing. 3. Biofilm Disruption: eNAP-2 - Equine neutrophil antimicrobial peptide that inhibits microbial serine proteases and biofilms. 4. Innate Immunity: eDEFB1 - Equine beta-defensin-1 providing a first-line barrier against Gram-positive pathogens. 5. Iron Sequestration: eLactoferricin-Eq - Equine-specific lactoferrin fragment that starves bacteria of essential iron. 6. Enhanced Drainage: Abscess-Flux-1 - Designed hybrid of eCATH and eNAP-2 for synergistic bacterial clearance and reduced viscosity. presets.name: Horse Hypertrichosis (C)"
presets.description: Horse Hypertrichosis for Contact  Horse hypertrichosis refers to excessive hair growth in equines, commonly a clinical sign of pituitary pars intermedia dysfunction (PPID), a neurodegenerative endocrine disorder in older horses involving loss of dopaminergic inhibition, excessive ACTH and POMC-derived peptide secretion, cortisol dysregulation, and secondary complications like insulin resistance and laminitis. The proposed polypeptides address therapeutic axes including restoration of dopaminergic inhibition (TRH, D1), suppression of ACTH production (Cosyntropin, D2), regulation of cortisol metabolism (ACTH, D3), normalization of hair follicle cycle ({alpha}-MSH, D4), improvement of insulin sensitivity (D5), neuroprotection of hypothalamic neurons (alpha-syn fragment 62-86, D6), modulation of gut microbiome (CLIP, D7), reduction of inflammation and laminitis prevention ({beta}-Endorphin, D8), and muscle mass preservation (D9-D13). Designed polypeptides (flagged as D) are conceptual, incorporating non-standard residues for enhanced stability, specificity, and bioavailability, with considerations for charge, hydrophobicity, and cyclic structures to optimize binding and pharmacokinetics. presets.name: Horse Kissing Spine (C)"
presets.description: Horse Kissing Spine for Contact  Horse Kissing Spine, or Overriding Dorsal Spinous Processes (ORDSP), occurs when the bony projections (spinous processes) on the vertebrae of a horse's back touch, rub, or overlap. This contact causes chronic pain, localized inflammation, and secondary muscular atrophy. Most common in the thoracic and lumbar regions (T13-T18), the condition leads to behavioral issues, poor performance, and restricted mobility.  1. Osteoclast Inhibition: eSalmon-Calcitonin - Inhibits bone resorption and reduces subchondral bone pain. 2. Anti-Inflammatory: eBPC-157 - Promotes ligamentous healing and reduces inflammation in interspinous spaces. 3. Muscle Regeneration: eIGF-1(24-41) - Counteracts focal atrophy of the multifidus and longissimus dorsi muscles. 4. Analgesic Signaling: Eq-Enkephalin - Targets local opioid receptors to manage chronic spinal nociception. 5. Cartilage Repair: ePentosan-Mimetic - Stimulates glycosaminoglycan synthesis in the false joints between processes. 6. Neuro-Muscular Axis: Spinous-Sync-1 - Optimized hybrid peptide for vertebral bone stabilization and nerve sheath protection. presets.name: Horse Navicular Syndrome (C)"
presets.description: Horse Navicular Syndrome for Contact  Horse Navicular Syndrome (HNS), also known as podotrochlear syndrome, is a chronic degenerative disease complex of the equine distal forelimb. It involves the navicular bone (distal sesamoid), the navicular bursa, and the deep digital flexor tendon (DDFT). The condition is characterized by an imbalance between mechanical loading and the structural integrity of the navicular apparatus, leading to subchondral bone remodeling, fibrocartilage erosion, and chronic inflammation.  1. IL-1 Antagonism: IL-1RA-Eq15 - Inhibition of IL-1 receptor signaling to reduce bursa and tendon inflammation. 2. Bone Remodeling: eTeriparatide - Anabolic stimulation of osteoblasts to repair navicular bone cystic lesions. 3. Soft Tissue Repair: eIGF-1(1-20) - Promotes fibrocartilage and DDFT healing via type II collagen synthesis. 4. Chondrogenesis: eBMP-2 Knuckle - Induces differentiation of synovial progenitor cells into chondrocytes. 5. Vascularization: EqAngio-7 - Promotes neovascularization in ischemic areas of the navicular bone. 6. Matrix Protection: Naviculen-1 - Hybrid BMP-2/IGF-1 mimetic for simultaneous bone and cartilage stabilization. presets.name: Horse Sarcoids (C)"
presets.description: Horse Sarcoids for Contact  Horse sarcoids are the most common dermatological neoplasms in equids, representing locally aggressive, non-metastatic fibroblastic skin tumors. They are etiologically linked to infection by Bovine Papillomavirus types 1 and 2 (BPV-1, BPV-2), and more recently BPV-13. Clinical presentation is categorized into six distinct forms: occult (flat/hairless), verrucose (wart-like), nodular (firm lumps), fibroblastic (fleshy/ulcerated), mixed, and the rare, highly invasive malevolent form. Current therapeutic strategies in 2024-2026 emphasize multi-modal approaches, including high-frequency irreversible electroporation (H-FIRE), viral vector-based immunotherapies (delNS technology), and peptide-based vaccines targeting the viral oncoproteins E5, E6, and E7 which maintain the transformed phenotype.  1. Viral Oncoprotein Targeting: BPV-1 E6-derived Peptide (iNSA/E6E7equ) - Immunogenic peptide fragment used in viral vector systems to induce CD8+ T-cell responses against tumor-expressing E6 antigens. 2. Viral Oncoprotein Targeting: BPV-1 E7-derived Peptide - Antigenic sequence designed to trigger systemic antitumor responses, specifically targeting BPV-transformed fibroblasts. 3. Oncogenic Pathway Inhibition: E5-Blocking Peptide - A synthetic sequence designed to disrupt the BPV-1 E5 oncoprotein's interaction with the PDGF receptor, inhibiting cell hyperproliferation. 4. Innate Immune Activation: eNAP-2 (Equine Neutrophil Antimicrobial Peptide) - A natural equine cathelicidin-like peptide that serves as an endogenous antibiotic and immune modulator against viral-associated lesions. 5. Antiviral Enzymatic Support: Lysozyme-based Peptide - A recombinant or synthetic fragment of equine lysozyme showing activity against papillomavirus by disrupting viral entry and replication. 6. Tumor Suppressor Support: p53-Stabilizing Peptide - A designed sequence that counteracts E6-mediated downregulation of p53-related pathways, promoting apoptosis in sarcoid cells. 7. Adhesion Disruption: Paxillin-mimetic Peptide - Engineered peptide that competitively binds to the E6 oncoprotein to prevent its interference with focal adhesion proteins, reducing tissue invasiveness. 8. Optimization Axis: Sarcoid-Regress-V1 - An engineered polypeptide combining BPV-antigens with a cell-penetrating motif (TAT) to enhance intracellular delivery and MHC-I presentation for complete lesion clearance. presets.name: Horse Strangles (C)"
presets.description: Horse Strangles for Contact  Horse strangles is a highly contagious upper respiratory tract infection caused by the bacterium *Streptococcus equi subspecies equi* (*S. equi*). It is characterized by sudden onset of fever, followed by mucopurulent nasal discharge and the swelling and subsequent abscessation of the submandibular and retropharyngeal lymph nodes. In severe cases, the swelling can become so pronounced that it obstructs the horse's airway, leading to the 'strangles' name. Recent research from 2024-2026 has focused on overcoming the limitations of current modified-live vaccines, specifically by developing multi-component subunit vaccines like Strangvac and identifying novel immunogenic peptides through ORFeome phage display to target the bacteria's evasive mechanisms.  1. Virulence Factor Inhibition: SeM-N (N-terminal Fragment) - A key antigenic peptide from the M-like protein of *S. equi* that inhibits phagocytosis; targeting this region helps restore the host's ability to clear bacteria. 2. Cell Wall Adhesion: CNE-derived Peptide - A collagen-binding protein fragment designed to block the bacteria's ability to adhere to the hosts extracellular matrix in the upper respiratory tract. 3. Immune Evasion Blockade: EAG-mimetic Peptide - A sequence that targets the IgG and albumin-binding protein of *S. equi*, preventing the bacteria from 'cloaking' itself from the equine immune system. 4. Mucosal Immunity Induction: Strangvac 4 (Component-8) - A specific recombinant fusion peptide used in intramuscular subunit vaccines to provide high levels of protection without the risk of abscessation associated with live vaccines. 5. Inflammatory Modulation: eNAP-1 (Equine Neutrophil Antimicrobial Peptide) - A natural equine peptide that provides rapid-onset antibacterial activity and modulates the massive neutrophil influx to reduce tissue damage. 6. Diagnostic & Vaccine Candidate: Proline-Rich Repeat Protein (PRP-S1) - A highly conserved surface peptide identified in 2025 as a robust candidate for universal *S. equi* protection across global strains. 7. Enzymatic Degradation: Serine Peptidase Fragment (SP-S2) - An immunogenic peptide derived from *S. equi* surface enzymes, used to induce antibodies that neutralize the bacteria's tissue-degrading capabilities. 8. Optimization Axis: Strangles-Shield-V1 - An engineered polypeptide designed as a multivalent antigen, combining conserved epitopes from SeM, CNE, and SEQ_0402 to provide a broad-spectrum, non-infectious vaccine response. presets.name: Horse Sweet Itch (C)"
presets.description: Horse Sweet Itch for Contact  Horse Sweet Itch, known clinically as Insect Bite Hypersensitivity (IBH) or Culicoides Hypersensitivity, is a chronic, relapsing seasonal allergic dermatitis caused by an IgE-mediated response to salivary proteins from biting midges (Culicoides spp.). In 2024-2026, therapeutic advances have shifted from mere symptom management to 'biologic-like' active vaccinations that neutralize the master regulators of the equine allergic cascade. These therapies aim to break the cycle of intense pruritus (itching), self-trauma, and chronic skin thickening (lichenification) that affects approximately 8-10% of horses globally.  1. Eosinophil Regulation: eIL-5-CuMVTT - A virus-like particle (VLP)-based vaccine that induces auto-antibodies against equine Interleukin-5, the master regulator of eosinophil survival and recruitment to the skin. 2. Pruritus Blockade: eIL-31-CuMVTT - A therapeutic vaccine targeting Interleukin-31, a key cytokine responsible for transmitting the 'itch' signal directly to peripheral nerves. 3. Allergen Immunotherapy (AIT): r-Culicoides Cocktail - A mixture of recombinant Culicoides salivary proteins (e.g., Cul o 2, Cul o 3) designed to induce IgG-mediated immune deviation and IgE blocking activity. 4. Mucosal Tolerance: Transgenic Barley-Cul o 2p - An innovative oral peptide delivery system using transgenic barley to induce mucosal tolerance and regulatory T-cell responses against major midge allergens. 5. Chemokine Sequestration: CCL11/Eotaxin-1 Fragment - A designed peptide fragment that competitively binds to the CCR3 receptor, preventing eosinophil migration into the dermis during the peak midge season. 6. JAK/STAT Signaling Inhibition: SOCS3-mimetic Peptide - A synthetic peptide that mimics the Suppressor of Cytokine Signaling 3 to intracellularly dampen the Th2-driven inflammatory response in keratinocytes. 7. Skin Barrier Repair: Desmoglein-1 stabilizing peptide - An engineered sequence designed to enhance intercellular adhesion in the equine epidermis, reducing the penetration of salivary allergens and secondary bacterial infections. 8. Optimization Axis: Sweet-Itch-Shield-V1 - A multivalent engineered polypeptide combining IL-5/IL-31 neutralizing epitopes with a Culicoides-specific IgG4/7-inducing sequence for comprehensive seasonal protection. presets.name: Horse Vaccine Detox (C)"
presets.description: Horse Vaccine Detox for Contact  Horse Vaccine Detox refers to the mitigation of adverse effects following equine immunization, such as localized swelling, lethargy, or systemic inflammatory responses like purpura hemorrhagica. While the term 'detox' is common in holistic veterinary contexts, clinical management focuses on modulating the innate immune response, chelating potential vaccine components (such as aluminum adjuvants), and reducing pro-inflammatory cytokine cascades. Therapeutic peptides in this axis are designed to suppress excessive Th2-driven eosinophilia, inhibit TSH-like cross-reactivity, and provide antioxidant support to neutralize oxidative stress at the injection site.  1. Cytokine Modulation: Equine IL-5 Fragment - Truncated sequence of equine interleukin-5 designed to competitively inhibit eosinophil activation and allergic sensitivity. 2. Innate Immune Regulation: SOCS1-mimetic (ss-SOCS1) - Peptide mimetic of the Suppressor of Cytokine Signaling 1 used to dampen JAK/STAT-mediated inflammatory storms post-vaccination. 3. Adjuvant Sequestration: Poly-L-Histidine - Synthetic polypeptide capable of chelating metal-based adjuvants like aluminum hydroxide through imidazole-metal coordination. 4. Inflammation Suppression: Thymosin alpha-1 - Highly conserved peptide that modulates T-cell function and reduces systemic inflammation while maintaining appropriate vaccine efficacy. 5. Oxidative Stress Support: Glutathione-mimetic (GSH-P) - Designed tripeptide derivative that enhances the horse's endogenous antioxidant capacity to combat vaccine-induced ROS. 6. Vascular Protection: Annexin A1-derived peptide (Ac2-26) - Peptide that mimics the anti-inflammatory effects of Annexin A1 to protect against necrotizing vasculitis and purpura. 7. Endocrine Support: Protirelin (TRH) - Synthetic tripeptide used to stabilize the hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis, which can be disrupted by systemic immune challenges. 8. Optimization Axis: EquiDetox-M1 - Engineered peptide optimized for high-affinity binding to equine-specific inflammatory markers (CCL11/Eotaxin) to prevent localized granuloma formation.
